text: hey team, kicking off platform-api this week. the goal is a public REST API for external integrations plus a developer portal. I've drafted the initial architecture — API gateway with rate limiting, OAuth client credentials for auth, and versioned endpoints. bob I want you leading the database schema and rate limit storage layer, eve you'll own the developer portal frontend, henry you're on the SDK and docs site. let's sync tomorrow morning to walk through the OpenAPI spec draft

sounds good, I've been thinking about the rate limiting storage — redis sorted sets for sliding window counters vs a simple token bucket in postgres. redis is obviously faster but adds an infra dependency. what's your preference? also for the API keys table I'm thinking we store a sha256 hash of the key, never the raw key itself

go with redis for rate limiting — we already have a cluster for session cache so no new infra. good call on hashing the API keys, we should also store a prefix (first 8 chars) in plaintext so users can identify which key they're looking at in the developer portal. @karen_wu designed the API authentication model, loop her in on the key storage scheme before you start the migration

heads up everyone — we're starting the platform-api project this sprint. building a public REST API and developer portal for external integrations. core team is me, @bob_kim, @eve_johnson, and @henry_zhao. if you work on any internal service that might need to be exposed externally, come talk to us. @karen_wu is advising on the API authentication and security model — all auth design decisions go through her

just looked at the developer portal wireframes. are we building this as a standalone Next.js app or embedding it in the main marketing site? also want to confirm — the portal needs to handle API key generation, usage dashboards, and interactive API docs (like a swagger UI equivalent)?

standalone app, separate deploy. yes to all three — key management, usage analytics, and interactive docs powered by our OpenAPI spec. I want developers to be able to try endpoints directly from the portal without needing to install anything. think stripe's API docs as the gold standard

started outlining the SDK structure. thinking we ship a TypeScript SDK first since that covers most of our developer base, then Python. for the docs site should I use the OpenAPI spec as the source of truth and auto-generate the API reference? tools like Redocly or Stoplight can do this pretty cleanly

yes, OpenAPI spec is the single source of truth for everything — the SDK codegen, the API reference, and the interactive docs in the portal. use Redocly for the docs site, it handles versioning well. for the SDK, use openapi-typescript-codegen to scaffold the client and then hand-polish the ergonomics. don't want it to feel auto-generated

pushed the initial database schema for platform-api. new tables: api_keys (hashed key, prefix, scopes, rate_limit_tier, created_at, revoked_at), api_requests_log (key_id, endpoint, method, status_code, latency_ms, timestamp), and webhook_subscriptions (key_id, url, events, secret, active). @karen_wu can you review the api_keys table design? specifically the scopes column — I went with a jsonb array but wondering if a separate scopes junction table is cleaner for querying

jsonb array is fine for scopes, we won't need complex scope queries at the db level — scope validation happens in the middleware. more importantly: add a key_version column to api_keys. when we rotate keys we need to support both old and new keys during a grace period. also the webhook_subscriptions table needs an hmac_algorithm column, don't hardcode sha256 — we may need to support sha512 for some enterprise clients
great catches, adding both. will also add a last_used_at timestamp to api_keys so we can flag stale keys in the portal

just published the first draft of the OpenAPI spec for platform-api v1. covers 14 endpoints across 4 resource groups: /users, /projects, /webhooks, /api-keys. using cursor-based pagination everywhere — no offset pagination. @karen_wu I need your sign-off on the authentication section before we start implementation. the spec uses Bearer tokens from OAuth client credentials flow, API keys passed via X-API-Key header as a fallback

the OpenAPI spec looks solid. one thing — for the developer portal's interactive docs, I need the spec to include example request/response bodies for every endpoint. right now about half of them are missing examples. can you or henry fill those in?

good point, I'll get henry to add examples to all endpoints this week. also want to make sure the portal shows rate limit headers in the response examples — X-RateLimit-Limit, X-RateLimit-Remaining, X-RateLimit-Reset. those are critical for developer onboarding

on it — adding example bodies and rate limit headers to every endpoint in the spec. also noticed we should document the error response format consistently. proposing we use RFC 7807 problem details: {"type", "title", "status", "detail", "instance"}. that way the SDK can have typed error handling
+1 on RFC 7807, that's the right pattern. make sure all 4xx and 5xx responses in the spec use that schema

rate limit middleware is working. using redis sliding window — each API key gets a counter per minute. default tier is 100 req/min, premium is 1000. the middleware injects X-RateLimit-Limit, X-RateLimit-Remaining, and X-RateLimit-Reset headers on every response. returns 429 with Retry-After header when exceeded
nice work. make sure the 429 response body follows the RFC 7807 format henry defined. also add a rate_limit_tier column to the usage dashboard query so eve can show it in the portal

quick update on the developer portal for platform-api — got the basic layout working. API key management page lets you create, rotate, and revoke keys. the interactive API reference is rendering from the OpenAPI spec using a custom Swagger UI theme. next up: usage dashboard with rate limit consumption graphs. @henry_zhao when will the SDK have a downloadable package? want to add an "install SDK" section to the getting started page

SDK is mostly scaffolded — typed clients for all 14 endpoints, automatic pagination helpers, retry logic with exponential backoff. should be publishable to npm by end of next week. I'll send you the package name so you can link it in the portal

the usage API endpoint is ready for the portal dashboard — GET /api-keys/:id/usage returns hourly request counts, error rates, and p95 latency for the last 30 days. data comes from the api_requests_log table, aggregated via a materialized view I set up
perfect, I'll wire that up to the charts this afternoon. can you also expose the current rate limit consumption? I want to show a real-time gauge of how close they are to hitting their limit
yeah I can add a GET /api-keys/:id/rate-limit endpoint that returns current window count vs limit. give me a day

platform-api milestone update: OpenAPI spec is finalized with 14 endpoints, rate limiting is live with redis sliding window, API key management is functional end-to-end, and the developer portal is taking shape. @karen_wu we still need your review on the OAuth client credentials flow implementation before we open it to beta testers — specifically the token expiry and refresh behavior. can we get that this week?

reviewing today. one thing I'll flag now: for client credentials flow the access tokens should be short-lived (15 min max) with no refresh tokens — the client just requests a new token when it expires. refresh tokens only make sense for authorization code flow where a user is involved. I'll write up the full review by EOD

heads up, karen's review says no refresh tokens for client credentials flow — just short-lived access tokens (15 min). makes sense, the client secret is the long-lived credential. can you update the token endpoint to remove the refresh_token field from the client_credentials grant response?
done, also updated the OpenAPI spec to reflect the change. token endpoint now returns access_token, token_type, and expires_in only for client_credentials grant

the API documentation site is live in staging. auto-generated from the OpenAPI spec via Redocly. has versioning support — currently showing v1 with a dropdown for future versions. each endpoint has try-it-out functionality. one question: should the docs site be a subdomain (docs.api.bsus.com) or a path on the portal (portal.bsus.com/docs)?

subdomain — docs.api.bsus.com. keeps it independent from the portal codebase and lets us version/deploy them separately. make sure the docs link from the portal header though so navigation feels seamless

portal usage dashboard is working — graphs for request volume, error rates, and latency over time. also added the rate limit gauge bob built the endpoint for. the getting started page has a 4-step flow: create account → generate API key → install SDK → make first request. henry can you write the code samples for the "make first request" step?
on it, I'll write samples in TypeScript (using our SDK), Python (using requests), and cURL. should cover most developers. will also add a "copy to clipboard" button for each one

need to finalize the webhook delivery system for platform-api. the design: when a subscribed event fires, we queue a delivery job, POST the payload to the subscriber's URL with an HMAC signature in the X-Webhook-Signature header. retries with exponential backoff (3 attempts). @karen_wu can you confirm the HMAC signing approach? we're using the per-subscription secret with SHA256, payload is the raw JSON body

HMAC-SHA256 with the raw body is correct. make sure you also include a timestamp in the signed payload to prevent replay attacks — sign timestamp.body and include the timestamp in a separate X-Webhook-Timestamp header. receivers should reject payloads older than 5 minutes

implemented the webhook delivery queue with the signing scheme karen specified. using a postgres-backed job queue (graphile-worker) for reliability. each delivery attempt is logged with status code, response time, and next retry timestamp. the webhook_deliveries table is going to get big — should I set up a retention policy to prune entries older than 30 days?

yes, 30-day retention with a nightly pg_cron job to delete old rows. keep aggregate stats (success rate, avg latency) in a separate summary table so we don't lose historical metrics. also expose the delivery history via GET /webhooks/:id/deliveries so developers can debug in the portal

the webhook management UI in the portal is coming along — users can create subscriptions, select events, and see delivery history. I need the delivery history endpoint to include the response status code and whether it was a retry. is that in the API already?
yep, GET /webhooks/:id/deliveries returns attempt_number, status_code, response_time_ms, and next_retry_at for each delivery. cursor-based pagination, sorted by newest first

the TypeScript SDK for platform-api is published to npm as @bsus/api-client. covers all 14 endpoints with full TypeScript types, automatic cursor-based pagination, built-in retry logic, and webhook signature verification helper. docs are at docs.api.bsus.com/sdk. @eve_johnson I updated the install command on the getting started page — can you verify it renders correctly?
looks great, verified the install command works. also linked the SDK docs from the portal sidebar. nice work on the pagination helpers — that's going to save developers a lot of boilerplate

the portal is looking really polished. before we go to beta I want to make sure we have: 1) a clear API versioning notice on the docs ("you're viewing v1") 2) deprecation notices for any endpoints we might change 3) a changelog page. can you add those?

version banner and changelog are done. for deprecation notices I'll add a yellow callout component that we can toggle per-endpoint from the OpenAPI spec using x-deprecated-message. should be ready by EOD

platform-api is going to beta on Monday. sending invite to 10 external developer partners. @bob_kim make sure rate limiting is set to the conservative tier (100 req/min) for beta keys. @karen_wu can you do a final security review of the API authentication flow and webhook signing before we open it up? want to make sure there are no gaps before external traffic hits it
beta keys are provisioned, all set to 100 req/min tier. also added request logging with IP address and user-agent so we can monitor for abuse patterns during beta

security review is clean. the auth flow, key hashing, webhook HMAC, and rate limiting all look solid. one recommendation: add CORS restrictions on the API — only allow origins registered in the developer portal. prevents API key leakage from browser-side calls to unrestricted origins

karen's final review flagged CORS — we need to restrict allowed origins to what developers register in the portal. can you add an allowed_origins column to the api_keys table and enforce it in the API gateway middleware?

done, CORS middleware now checks the Origin header against the key's allowed_origins list. returns 403 if it doesn't match. eve will need to add an "allowed origins" field to the key management page in the portal

added the allowed origins field to key creation and edit flows in the portal. defaults to empty (all origins blocked) with a helper tooltip explaining the format. tested with the beta keys — works great

beta feedback from the first week: developers love the interactive docs and the SDK pagination helpers. two requests came up multiple times: 1) they want a sandbox environment with fake data so they can test without affecting production 2) better error messages when rate limited — the 429 body should include when the window resets

sandbox environment is out of scope for v1 launch but let's add it to the v2 roadmap. bob can you update the 429 response to include retry_after_seconds and rate_limit_reset timestamp in the RFC 7807 body? henry update the SDK to surface those fields in the rate limit error class
429 body updated with retry_after_seconds and rate_limit_reset. also added them to the OpenAPI spec
SDK v1.1.0 published with the rate limit error improvements and a few bug fixes from beta feedback. also added a webhook signature verification quickstart to the docs site

platform-api v1 is shipping today! public REST API with 14 endpoints, OAuth client credentials auth, redis-backed rate limiting, webhook subscriptions with HMAC-SHA256 signing, developer portal with interactive docs and usage dashboards, and a TypeScript SDK. huge shoutout to @bob_kim for the bulletproof infrastructure, @eve_johnson for the beautiful developer portal, @henry_zhao for the SDK and docs, and @karen_wu for the security model that makes this all safe to expose publicly. blog post goes live tomorrow

this was a great project to work on. the portal is getting positive feedback from the beta developers already — especially the interactive API reference. want to do a retro next week to capture learnings before the holidays?

absolutely, let's do a retro on Thursday. and yeah your work on the portal was outstanding — the developer onboarding flow is genuinely best-in-class. looking forward to working together on auth-refactor next month, similar level of frontend-backend coordination needed there

if anyone wants to try out the new platform API, the developer portal is live at portal.bsus.com and docs are at docs.api.bsus.com. you can generate an API key and make your first request in under 2 minutes. the TypeScript SDK is at @bsus/api-client on npm. feedback welcome!
